baseline_dominance.justification: The construction identifies real gaps vs. the baseline: adversarial-AS soundness with circuit-enforced implication closure (G6), and ZK predicate evaluation over hidden bitmask (G5). However, multiple Tier 3 personas successfully narrow the dominance claim. The auth0_pm correctly notes the adversarial-AS advantage collapses in single-org deployments (the majority of the market), and the construction concedes this in Section 3. The rfc7662_advocate shows jwt-introspection-response achieves offline RS verification, reducing AS-blindness to a non-unique property. G6 enforces only 3 of 28 possible pairwise implications for the documented 8-bit space, and the rfc7662_advocate correctly notes an AS policy table enforcing the same 3 rules achieves identical semantic coverage — the advantage is only that the AS cannot bypass G6, not that G6 covers more. The baseline can approximate the core value proposition (boolean-return cached JWT + AS policy enforcing the same 3 implications) with extra configuration, but cannot match the adversarial-AS + implication-closure combination in multi-party settings. This is preferable, not provably impossible for the baseline to approximate.

formal_security_argument.justification: The construction provides an explicit threat model (Section 3) with named adversary capabilities (controls AS, network, up to k-1 governors, up to N-1 agents, chosen-predicate), three formal security games (SelectiveScopeForgery, RogueEnrollmentForgery, ImplicationClosureForgery), a privacy game (mqSE-IND), seven named cryptographic assumptions (PLONK knowledge soundness, Poseidon collision resistance, DL on Baby Jubjub, EdDSA-Poseidon unforgeability, ROM, PLONK SE, ECDSA unforgeability), and reduction sketches for soundness, enrollment integrity, and privacy. The cryptographer's Attack 2 (SRS provenance) identifies that pot16.ptau provenance is unnamed, but the reduction sketch is structurally complete — the SRS integrity is implicitly covered by Assumption 1 (PLONK knowledge soundness with universal SRS). The privacy game weakness (cryptographer Attack 1, rfc7662_advocate Attack 4, spiffe_engineer Attack 2) — chosen-predicate full bitmask recovery in 64 queries — is a real limitation, but the construction's mqSE-IND game is formally sound as stated; the gap is that the game is too weak, not that the reduction is wrong. The formal apparatus meets the bar: explicit threat model, named assumptions, stated reduction sketches.

implementability.justification: Section 5 maps every construction element to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon over BN254, EdDSAPoseidonVerifier, BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH=20), PLONK with pot16.ptau. Section 6 provides a detailed constraint-count estimate (~18,150 total), well within the 2^16 pot16.ptau budget. Proving time targets are stated: <500ms rapidsnark, <3s snarkjs. Proof size: 768 bytes PLONK. Enrollment gas: ~150K. No new cryptographic primitives are introduced. The enrollment governance uses standard EIP-712 + ecrecover. The circuit is a restructuring of the existing AgentPolicy circuit. The auth0_pm's onboarding complexity attack and the cu_ciso's latency concerns are operational, not implementability issues — the circuit maps to existing primitives with stated cost within Phase 1 budget.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 codex adversary with 5 personas attacked the construction extensively. The construction survives on core cryptographic soundness (no persona broke the proof system or forged a scope proof). However, multiple personas identified attacks the construction cannot fully answer: (1) The cryptographer and spiffe_engineer and rfc7662_advocate all independently identified that chosen-predicate querying recovers the full bitmask in 64 queries — the mqSE-IND game is vacuously satisfied, and the privacy claim is information-theoretically broken against a malicious RS with unlimited query access. The construction's Section 2 defense ('matches any subset') is formally incorrect as the cryptographer demonstrates. (2) The cu_ciso identifies that incident response revocation requires a k-of-n governance ceremony with no emergency path — a real operational attack the construction flags as 'future work'. (3) The auth0_pm correctly identifies that the adversarial-AS advantage collapses in single-org deployments without the construction scoping this as a limitation. (4) The cryptographer identifies nullifier rate-limiting is incoherent — fresh blindingNonce makes per-agent rate limiting impossible, contradicting the claimed rate-limiting property. These weaken the claims materially even if the core soundness holds.

scenario_fit.justification: Section 7 names a concrete deployment: Columbia Credit Union ($3.4B assets, Vancouver WA), Pacific Northwest consortium of 12 credit unions, AI agent loan pre-qualification across federated data lake. The scenario identifies a load-bearing property: rival credit unions do not trust each other's AS to honestly assert agent permissions — the adversarial-AS property is essential, not nice-to-have, in the cross-CU data sharing context. The regulatory requirement is named: NCUA Letter to Credit Unions 24-CU-03 on third-party AI risk management, FFIEC 2025 API security guidance, GLBA Safeguards Rule. The cu_ciso's audit trail and vendor management attacks identify real gaps in the scenario's operational viability, but the scenario itself is concrete with named stakeholders and regulatory requirements that make the property load-bearing.

gaps: Privacy claim is information-theoretically broken: a chosen-predicate RS recovers the full 64-bit bitmask in exactly 64 queries. The mqSE-IND game is vacuously satisfied (B0=B1 forced after exhaustive queries). Must either add a protocol-level query-count bound (e.g., agentNullifier-based rate limit < bitmask width per session), or restate the privacy claim as 'per-presentation ZK, not per-session ZK against malicious RS'.

Adversarial-AS advantage collapses in single-org deployments (majority of market). Section 3's parenthetical concession must be promoted to a first-class scoping statement: Properties 1-5 are distinguishing only in multi-party cross-institutional deployments.

Nullifier rate-limiting is incoherent: fresh blindingNonce per presentation makes agentNullifier unlinkable across presentations, so RS cannot associate multiple presentations with the same agent. Either remove the rate-limiting claim or specify a deterministic nullifier family (e.g., RS-supplied session nonce replaces agent-chosen blindingNonce in the nullifier computation).

Revocation has no emergency path: Phase 1 revocation requires k-of-n governance ceremony + full tree re-enrollment. No maximum staleness bound, no emergency single-governor freeze. Must specify either (a) an emergency revocation mechanism or (b) mandatory short TTLs that bound blast radius without governance ceremony.

SRS provenance unspecified: pot16.ptau is cited without naming the ceremony (Hermez PoT vs. snarkjs dev artifact). Must pin to a specific ceremony with verifiable transcript hash, or add SRS integrity as a named assumption.

G6 implication closure covers only 3 of 28 possible pairwise implications for the 8-bit space. Must enumerate all enforced implications and acknowledge the AS-policy comparison holds only for those specific edges. The 64-bit scalability claim needs constraint-count analysis for the target implication DAG.

No examiner-facing audit disclosure mechanism: on-chain logs contain only Poseidon hashes. Must specify a regulator-facing disclosure path (e.g., verifiable audit credential) that binds credentialCommitment to plaintext parameters without undermining ZK properties for other parties.

jwt-introspection-response achieves offline RS verification — Section 8 Property 3 overstates AS-blindness as unique. Reframe the differentiator as 'RS does not trust the AS at all' rather than 'AS not in the hot path'.
